info.topictitle: B2.1.17 Adhesion of cells to form tissues (HL)

markdown:
## Cells Rely on Cell-Adhesion Molecules to Form Tissues


1. Cells within tissues are interconnected through **cell-to-cell junctions**, which rely on **cell-adhesion molecules (CAMs)** found in the plasma membranes of neighboring cells. 
2. These junctions are crucial for maintaining the **structure **and** function** of tissues and organs.

<definition term="Cell-adhesion molecules (CAMs)">**Cell-adhesion molecules (CAMs)** are proteins that span the phospholipid bilayer of the membrane.</definition>

![Cell-to-cell adhesion](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/86e7914940a994a33c7559fdba6356115ded9e81-803x436.jpg)

<callout type="note">While you are not required to have detailed knowledge of individual CAM types, understanding their general role in cell adhesion and tissue formation is essential.</callout>

## Structure and Function of CAMs


1. Certain domains of CAMs are embedded within the bilayer, while others protrude into the extracellular environment.
2. **Junction formation** occurs when CAMs on adjacent cells bind together through their extracellular domains.
  1. In some cases, the **same **CAM type is present in both cells, facilitating **symmetrical junctions** that group similar cell types.
  2. In other cases, different CAMs interact to form **asymmetrical junctions**, allowing for connections between **distinct** cell types and creating more **complex** tissue structures.

<callout type="hint">CAMs are highly specific to the cells they bind to, ensuring proper tissue organization</callout>

## Importance of Cell Adhesion


1. **Cell adhesion maintains tissue architecture.** 
2. It ensures structural integrity and facilitates functional relationships between adjacent cells.
3. Certain junctions:
  1. **Prevent extracellular movement** of substances, maintaining compartmentalization in tissues.
  2. **Facilitate controlled transport** of substances when necessary.
4. **In the immune system:**
  1. CAMs help distinguish between self and foreign cells, a critical function in immune responses.
  2. **In cancer biology**, loss of adhesion can lead to metastasis, as cells detach from the primary tumor and migrate to form secondary tumors.

<callout type="example">In the heart, gap junctions allow electrical signals to travel rapidly across cardiac cells, ensuring that the heart beats as a coordinated unit.</callout>

<callout type="warning">* Students often confuse cell adhesion with the extracellular matrix (ECM). 
* While both contribute to tissue structure, CAMs** directly link** cells to each other, whereas the ECM provides a scaffold for cells to attach to.</callout>

<callout type="hint">When CAMs fail, the risk of malignancy spreading through metastasis increases.</callout>
